ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for mazesoba or mapo-style dry noodles. A0FJP (Noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for mazesoba or mapo mazesoba. No defensible parent code identified in clinical terminology for this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of mapo mazesoba with special toppings, approximately 450 g, consisting of wheat noodles, mapo tofu sauce with minced pork, and additional toppings such as chashu, soft-boiled egg, and nori.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: wheat noodles (cooked), pork (ground, cooked), firm tofu, and spicy sauce components; proportions modelled on standard mazesoba recipe composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mapo mazesoba exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated by modelling the dish from its primary components using USDA FoodData Central data. Assumed composition per 100 g of finished dish: approximately 45% cooked wheat noodles, 20% cooked ground pork, 15% silken tofu, 10% sauce (doubanjiang, soy sauce, sesame oil), 10% toppings (egg, chashu, nori). Macros are weighted averages of component nutrient profiles. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles (cooked) used as analogous estimate for mazesoba noodle base; GI approximately 47 for Asian-style wheat noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mapo mazesoba. The GI of 47 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked Asian wheat noodles (GI ~47, low category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The mapo tofu sauce and protein/fat content further attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (47 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 37.0, rounded to 36.8 using 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 450 g serving / 100 = 78.75 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from doubanjiang, soy sauce, and chashu toppings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid mapo mazesoba due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 2340 mg per bowl), which substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium target of 600–800 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles, partially offset by protein, fat, and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with mapo mazesoba as the estimated glycemic load per bowl is approximately 37, which is high; portion control is essential, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables may help moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium, saturated fat from minced pork and chashu, and cholesterol from egg topping
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of mapo mazesoba due to its combination of high sodium (~2340 mg per bowl), saturated fat (~9.5 g per bowl), and cholesterol (~189 mg per bowl), all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mapo mazesoba with special toppings is a Japanese brothless dry noodle dish that fuses the flavours of Chinese mapo tofu with the mazesoba (mixed noodle) format popularised in Japan. The dish consists of thick wheat noodles served without broth, topped with a rich, spicy sauce made from doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), minced pork, silken tofu, soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and Sichuan peppercorn. The 'special toppings' typically include chashu braised pork, a soft-boiled marinated egg (ajitsuke tamago), and nori seaweed. The diner mixes all components together before eating, coating the noodles in the intensely savoury, numbing-spicy sauce. The texture profile combines chewy noodles, silky tofu, and tender pork, with a complex umami-forward flavour profile accented by the characteristic mala (numbing-spicy) sensation of Sichuan peppercorn. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~168 kcal per 100 g), provides meaningful protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) from pork and tofu, and contains moderate carbohydrate (~17.5 g per 100 g) primarily from wheat noodles. The glycemic index is estimated at 47 (low category) based on the wheat noodle base, though the glycemic load per full bowl is high (~37) due to portion size. The dish is clinically notable for its high sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g, approximately 2340 mg per bowl), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The saturated fat and cholesterol from pork and egg toppings also warrant attention for cardiovascular risk management. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without modification.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?
What does mabou mazesoba taste like?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mapo mazesoba exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by component modelling using USDA FoodData Central data for cooked wheat noodles, ground pork, silken tofu, and sauce ingredients. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Asian wheat noodles as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation. All other -1 fields have been replaced with estimated values where component data permitted. Nutrient values are modelled estimates based on assumed dish composition (45% cooked wheat noodles, 20% cooked ground pork, 15% silken tofu, 10% sauce components, 10% special toppings) and should be validated against laboratory analysis or a direct approved-source entry if one becomes available. The dish is served at Ramen Matsuri, 7 North Canal Road, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data (seed record GD002601). Price at time of data collection: SGD 22.68. The Chinese script entries in aliases_by_language reflect the Japanese kanji used in the dish name (麻婆まぜそば) rather than a separate Chinese-language name for the dish; this is consistent with the Japanese cuisine classification. No Singaporean-cuisine multilingual requirements apply as this is classified as a Japanese dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile via USDA FoodData Central component modelling as no direct approved-source entry exists.
Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database entry for Asian wheat noodles as closest analogous food.
Added Japanese local name (麻婆まぜそば（特製トッピング付き）) and romanized form to satisfy G6 Japanese cuisine requirement.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes as required by G3.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases list.
Set ontology match types to closest_parent for FoodOn and FoodEx2 with gap notes; SNOMED CT set to none with gap note.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three required health conditions with quantified values.
